why_lexical_misses: The algorithm lives only inside the compound OpenSSL cipher string 'des-ede3-cbc'; a \bDES\b or DES(?![\w-]) guard never fires because 'des' is glued to '-ede3-cbc' and '3DES'/'TripleDES' tokens never literally appear.

createHash is import-renamed to digestWith, so a scanner keyed on the createHash call name finds nothing; the algorithm 'sha1' is only a quoted string arg and word-boundary \bSHA1\b can match but the call-context (a hash) is invisible to a bare-token pass, yielding no actionable finding.

crypto is bound to local `c`, and the algorithm name is assembled at runtime as 'rc' + '4', so the literal token RC4 never appears in source for \bRC4\b to match; only constant-folding the concatenation reveals it.

The call site `quickDigest(...)` contains no crypto token at all; only a two-pass scan that links the call to the user-defined quickDigest body finds the 'md5' string. A lexical pass would flag the definition's 'md5' but cannot attribute the etag call site to a weak hash.

The signing algorithm is a quoted property value ('HS256'/'none') on an options object passed to jwt.sign, not a recognizable crypto-primitive token; \bRSA\b/\bMD5\b style matchers have no entry for 'HS256' or the catastrophic alg:'none', and a regex sees only opaque strings.

RSA and SHA1 are fused inside one OpenSSL digest-name string 'RSA-SHA1'; \bRSA\b can be tripped by the substring but \bSHA1\b is glued after a hyphen, and the scanner cannot tell this is an asymmetric signature (quantum-broken) versus an incidental mention without parsing the createSign chain.

The WebCrypto algorithm is specified as a nested object literal { name:'ECDSA', hash:{ name:'SHA-256' } }; the curve/primitive is a JSON-like value, and ECDSA is not in a typical \bRSA\b|\bDES\b|\bMD5\b token list, so a bare-token scanner produces no quantum-broken finding for the signature.

The weakness is the ECB mode of operation encoded inside 'aes-128-ecb'; a token list of broken ALGORITHMS (RSA/DES/MD5/RC4) has no entry for an insecure MODE, so the ECB usage (deterministic, leaks plaintext patterns) is invisible without parsing the cipher-spec string.

'DES-CBC' is a node-forge factory name string; a DES(?![\w-]) guard is defeated by the trailing '-CBC', and a scanner unaware of forge.cipher.createCipher cannot tell this string selects single-DES (56-bit, broken) rather than naming an unrelated constant.

The cipher name is read from an env var / config object at runtime, so no algorithm literal exists in source for any regex (or even constant-folding) to match; the actual algorithm — possibly 'des-cbc' or 'rc4' in deployment — is only knowable from runtime configuration, the genuine blind spot.

The exported signWebhook call shows no crypto token; resolving it requires linking through the arrow-function `mac` to the createHmac('md5', ...) body. A lexical pass might flag 'md5' but would mislabel it as a plain hash and never reach the webhook-signing call site that actually depends on the weak MAC.

Algorithm is the leading token of the compound transformation string "DES/CBC/PKCS5Padding"; a \bDES\b scanner that excludes slash-joined or transformation strings (or a DES(?![\w/]) guard) skips it, and the SecretKeySpec "DES" is also string-only.

3DES is spelled "DESede" in JCA, never the tokens "3DES"/"TripleDES"; a scanner keyed on \b3DES\b or \bDES\b with word boundaries misses it because "DESede" contains no standalone DES token (the 'e' defeats the right boundary).

Both primitives are fused inside one camelCase string "SHA1withRSA": there is no \bSHA1\b (it is glued to 'withRSA' with no boundary) and no \bRSA\b (preceded by 'with' with no boundary). A bare-token regex matching whole words finds neither.

The key type is the two-letter string "EC" (which \bEC\b would match only with EC-specific rules, and most scanners do not list bare "EC"), and the curve is hidden in "secp256r1"; the signing algorithm ECDSA is glued inside "SHA256withECDSA", so neither curve nor algorithm surfaces as a listed bare token.

The literal "MD5" never appears as a contiguous token; it is built by string concatenation "MD" + "5" into a local var, so \bMD5\b matches nothing. A structural pass can fold the constant concatenation and resolve digestAlg before the getInstance call.

The call site makeTransportKeys(2048) contains no crypto token at all; "RSA" lives only as a string return value of a separate helper asymAlgo(). A single-line regex sees neither RSA at the call nor a getInstance("RSA") literal; a 2-pass scan must link the wrapper body and the helper's return constant.

RC4 is registered under the SunJCE alias "ARCFOUR" (also "RC4"), so a \bRC4\b scanner never sees the token; additionally the cipher is hidden behind streamCipher(), so the call site has no algorithm string. Resolving requires both an alias table and wrapper-body linkage.

Two weak primitives are embedded inside the compound PBE name "PBEWithMD5AndDES": MD5 is glued between 'With' and 'And' and DES is a suffix, so neither \bMD5\b nor \bDES\b word-boundary match fires. The second Cipher.getInstance receives f.getAlgorithm() (a method-chain value), so its algorithm is invisible without dataflow back to the factory string.

The algorithm string is read from an external properties file at runtime (cfg.getProperty("cipher.transform")), so no algorithm token exists in the source at all; only by parsing the deployed prod.properties value can the DES transform be known, which a source-only scanner cannot resolve.

The key size 128 that distinguishes AES-128 (quantum-weakened) from AES-256 (quantum-safe) is set by a separate kg.init(128) call, not by the "AES" string; a lexical scanner sees only "AES" and cannot bind the keysize, so it cannot assign the correct quantum-weakened risk without dataflow linking init(128) to the AES generator.

snippet:
import hashlib

def file_fingerprint(data: bytes) -> str:
    # legacy cache key, kept for backwards compat
    algo = 'md5'
    h = hashlib.new(algo)
    h.update(data)
    return h.hexdigest()
why_lexical_misses: The token 'md5' is a lowercase quoted string bound to a variable, so a \bMD5\b case-sensitive word-boundary regex never fires.

A \bDES\b / DES(?![\w-]) guard is defeated: the real reference is 'TripleDES' (the substring 'DES' is glued to 'Triple' and to a trailing word char), and the OpenSSL alias 'des-ede3-cbc' has 'des' followed by '-', so both word-boundary forms miss.

hashlib is aliased to 'hl' and the constructor hl.sha1 is rebound to a local 'factory' before being called, so neither the import line nor the call site contains a recognizable bare SHA1 token next to a crypto module name.

The call site obfuscate()->_make_stream() contains no algorithm token; the real crypto is 'ARC4' (the pycryptodome name for RC4), which a \bRC4\b regex never matches because the spelling is ARC4. A 2-pass scan must link the user-defined wrapper body to its caller.

The signing call has no algorithm literal at the call site (the hash class is looked up via a dict keyed by a profile string and instantiated indirectly), and the RSA key is only an opaque 'private_key' parameter, so neither SHA1 nor RSA appears as a matchable token where the signing happens.

The module/class is 'DES3' (DES glued to a digit) and the chained constructor uses DES3.new(...).encrypt(...); a DES(?![\w-]) or \bDES\b guard rejects 'DES3' because 'DES' is immediately followed by the digit '3' (a word char).

The digest name 'sha1' is passed as a string into a factory that returns a closure, then bound to 'sign'; the actual auth_tag() call site shows neither HMAC nor SHA1, and the lowercase quoted 'sha1' defeats a \bSHA1\b token match. Linking requires tracking the string through the closure.

The algorithm class is resolved via getattr(algorithms, cfg.cipher_name) from a runtime config value, so no algorithm token exists in the source at all; only runtime data binding determines whether it is AES, TripleDES, etc.

The PBKDF2 PRF hash is read from an environment variable (PWHASH) with a default supplied via os.environ.get, so the effective algorithm is a runtime value; a static scanner cannot know whether it is 'sha1', 'sha256', etc., and the literal default is a lowercase string.

RSA is imported under the alias 'KeyPair' (from ... import RSA as KeyPair), so every subsequent use is KeyPair.generate/import_key with no 'RSA' token at the call sites; only the import line carries it, and a call-site-only scanner sees nothing.
The cipher suites appear only as bare hex uint16 IANA codepoints (0x000a/0xc012) with no DES/RSA/SHA token anywhere in the source.

Algorithm names are fused inside compound constant identifiers (RC4_128, ECDHE_RSA); a \bRC4\b / \bRSA\b word-boundary regex won't fire because the tokens are joined by underscores into one identifier.

snippet:
// signer.Hash configured from a numeric profile field; 2 == crypto.MD5
h := crypto.Hash(2).New()
h.Write(payload)
sig, _ := rsa.SignPKCS1v15(rand.Reader, priv, crypto.Hash(2), h.Sum(nil))
_ = sig
why_lexical_misses: MD5 is selected via the crypto.Hash enum ordinal crypto.Hash(2); the literal token 'MD5' never appears, so a name-token scan sees no hash algorithm at all.
Both algorithms are welded into one enum identifier x509.SHA1WithRSA; '\bSHA1\b' and '\bRSA\b' guards fail on the camel-cased compound token SHA1WithRSA.

snippet:
// curve chosen for a constrained device; P-224 is sub-128-bit security
curve := elliptic.P224()
priv, x, y, _ := elliptic.GenerateKey(curve, rand.Reader)
_ = priv; _ = x; _ = y
why_lexical_misses: The weak curve is named only via the constructor elliptic.P224(); there is no 'ECDSA'/'ECC'/'EC' token, so an algorithm-name regex never associates this with elliptic-curve crypto.
The crypto/md5 package is imported under the alias 'stdmd5' and used as stdmd5.Sum; a scanner keyed on the 'md5.' call-site prefix (or the bare token at the use site) misses the renamed binding.

The des.NewCipher call lives in newLegacyCipher; the call site encryptRecord uses only 'newLegacyCipher', so a single-pass token scan of the encryption path sees no DES — a 2-pass def/use link is required.

The 1024-bit key size flows from a map lookup keyed by a string label resolved at runtime; the literal '1024' never sits beside rsa.GenerateKey, so neither a token nor a constant-arg check classifies the weak key size.

snippet:
import _ "golang.org/x/crypto/md4"

// md4 registered via crypto.RegisterHash; selected through crypto.Hash value
var legacyHash = crypto.MD4
h := legacyHash.New()
h.Write(data)
digest := h.Sum(nil)
_ = digest
why_lexical_misses: The hash is bound to a variable (legacyHash := crypto.MD4) then invoked via .New(); a scanner matching the 'md4.New()' package-call shape never sees the package because the algorithm enters through the crypto.MD4 enum and a blank import.

snippet:
// algo name comes from operator-supplied config at startup
switch cfg.Cipher { // e.g. "rc4" loaded from config.yaml
case "rc4":
    c, _ = rc4.NewCipher(key)
case "aes":
    blk, _ := aes.NewCipher(key)
    c = cipher.StreamReader{S: cipher.NewCTR(blk, iv)}.S
}
_ = c
why_lexical_misses: Which branch executes depends on cfg.Cipher loaded from config.yaml at runtime; static analysis sees both rc4 and aes constructors but cannot decide which algorithm is actually selected in a given deployment.

snippet:
#!/bin/sh
# Re-wrap the legacy keystore for the nightly backup job
openssl enc -des-ede3-cbc -salt \
  -in /etc/pki/keystore.p12 \
  -out /var/backups/keystore.enc \
  -pass file:/etc/pki/.backup_pass
chmod 0400 /var/backups/keystore.enc
why_lexical_misses: The algorithm is the compound CLI flag '-des-ede3-cbc'; a \bDES\b or DES(?![\w-]) token regex never fires because 'des' is glued to 'ede3-cbc' with hyphens.

snippet:
# legacy interop: vendor appliance only accepts ARCFOUR streams
openssl enc -rc4 -K $(cat /opt/vendor/streamkey.hex) \
  -in payload.bin -out payload.rc4
# decrypt side mirrors this on the appliance
why_lexical_misses: '-rc4' is a leading-hyphen flag token, not a bare \bRC4\b word; a scanner keyed on word-boundary RC4 may also be tuned to skip option flags, and the '$()' command substitution noise distracts naive line matchers.

expected.algo: SHA-1 (SHA suffix MAC/PRF in cipher suites)

why_lexical_misses: The weak 3DES appears as the embedded token 'DES-CBC3' inside a colon-delimited OpenSSL cipher string; a \bDES\b boundary regex misfires because 'DES' is surrounded by '-' (a word char boundary is between 'RSA-' and 'DES', but most scanners exclude '!aNULL'-style cipher strings or only flag standalone DES). SHA-1 is named only as the bare suffix 'SHA' (not 'SHA1'), which a \bSHA1\b pattern never matches.

snippet:
<VirtualHost *:443>
    SSLEngine on
    SSLProtocol all -SSLv2
    SSLCipherSuite RC4-MD5:RC4-SHA:HIGH:!aNULL:!eNULL
    SSLCertificateFile    /etc/httpd/tls/legacy.pem
    SSLCertificateKeyFile /etc/httpd/tls/legacy.key
</VirtualHost>
why_lexical_misses: MD5 appears only as a hyphen-glued cipher-suite component 'RC4-MD5'; a \bMD5\b pattern with hyphen-as-boundary might catch it, but the riskier miss is SSLv3 — it is never named, it is implied by 'all -SSLv2' which only excludes SSLv2, leaving SSLv3 enabled. No literal weak-protocol token exists for a lexical scanner to match.

snippet:
# /etc/ssh/sshd_config — kept for old jump hosts
KexAlgorithms diffie-hellman-group14-sha1,diffie-hellman-group1-sha1
HostKeyAlgorithms ssh-rsa,ssh-dss
Ciphers aes128-cbc,3des-cbc
MACs hmac-sha1,hmac-md5
why_lexical_misses: DSA hides behind the SSH spelling 'ssh-dss' (never the token 'DSA'); 3DES is '3des-cbc' (starts with a digit, so \bDES\b fails and even \b3DES\b fails since it is '3des'); SHA-1 is the suffix '-sha1' on DH/MAC names, not a standalone \bSHA1\b. A token scanner keyed on canonical names misses all three.

snippet:
[ system_default_sect ]
MinProtocol = TLSv1.2
CipherString = DEFAULT@SECLEVEL=1
SignatureAlgorithms = RSA+SHA256:ECDSA+SHA1:RSA+SHA1
MaxProtocol = TLSv1.3
why_lexical_misses: SHA-1 appears as the structured suffix 'SHA1' joined by '+' inside a SignatureAlgorithms list ('ECDSA+SHA1'); a \bSHA1\b regex could fire, but the dominant miss is 'SECLEVEL=1' — a config directive that silently re-enables 80-bit/SHA-1/1024-bit RSA. No algorithm token names that downgrade; only structural understanding of the OpenSSL SECLEVEL semantics flags it.

snippet:
# pinned intermediate shipped with the appliance firmware
# Signature Algorithm: sha1WithRSAEncryption  (OID 1.2.840.113549.1.1.5)
# Public Key Algorithm: rsaEncryption, 1024 bit
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
MIIBxDCCAW6gAwIBAgIJAKZ7... (truncated body) ...Qn5lD1cLAEm9Cw==
-----END CERTIFICATE-----
why_lexical_misses: Inside an encoded PEM body the algorithm is only knowable from the ASN.1 OID '1.2.840.113549.1.1.5' (sha1WithRSAEncryption) or the camelCase identifier 'sha1WithRSAEncryption' — neither matches \bSHA1\b/\bSHA-1\b cleanly; the base64 cert body itself contains the real DER but is opaque to any token regex without an ASN.1 decode pass.

snippet:
global
    ssl-default-bind-ciphers ${TLS_CIPHER_PROFILE}
    ssl-default-bind-options no-sslv3

# TLS_CIPHER_PROFILE is injected at boot from Consul KV;
# the 'legacy-pci' profile resolves to 'AES128-SHA:DES-CBC3-SHA'
frontend fe_https
    bind :443 ssl crt /etc/haproxy/site.pem

expected.algo: SHA-1 (SHA suite MAC, resolved at runtime)

why_lexical_misses: The cipher list is the variable '${TLS_CIPHER_PROFILE}' resolved from Consul KV at boot; the actual weak suites ('DES-CBC3-SHA') never appear as literals in the config file, so no token regex — and no static AST pass — can see them without resolving external runtime state.

snippet:
# generate the signing key for the internal CA (kept from 2014 runbook)
openssl dsaparam -out dsaparam.pem 1024
openssl gendsa -out ca-signing.key dsaparam.pem
openssl req -new -x509 -key ca-signing.key \
  -sha1 -days 3650 -out ca.crt
why_lexical_misses: DSA is invoked through the subcommand names 'dsaparam' and 'gendsa' (a wrapper-style CLI verb), not the bare token 'DSA'; '\bDSA\b' never matches 'gendsa'/'dsaparam'. SHA-1 is the '-sha1' option flag, missed by \bSHA1\b word-boundary matching.

snippet:
# helper macro expanded by our config templater (jsonnet -> yaml)
# weak_tls_params() emits the block below for legacy partner endpoints
tls_params:
  tls_minimum_protocol_version: TLSv1_0
  cipher_suites:
    - "ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256"
    - "AES128-SHA"
    - "DES-CBC3-SHA"
why_lexical_misses: The weak settings live in a templater-generated block where 3DES is the embedded suite token 'DES-CBC3-SHA' and TLS 1.0 is the Envoy enum 'TLSv1_0' (underscore form, not 'TLS1.0'/'TLSv1.0'); neither matches \bDES\b nor a dotted TLS-version regex. Linking the emitted YAML back to the 'weak_tls_params()' macro needs a 2-pass def->use scan.
Algorithm lives inside the string "TripleDES" passed to CryptoConfig.CreateFromName; a \bDES\b matcher never fires and \bRSA\b/\bMD5\b don't apply.
The algorithm is identified only by the OID "1.3.14.3.2.7" (DES-CBC); no DES/MD5/RSA token appears anywhere in the source.
RSA is reached through cert.GetRSAPrivateKey() (a method-name substring, not the bare \bRSA\b token) and SHA-1 only appears inside the HashAlgorithmName("SHA1") string constructor.
A scanner keyed on MD5.Create() / new MD5CryptoServiceProvider() misses HashAlgorithm.Create(Algo) where MD5 is a const string bound to a local, not a type reference.
The call site MakeTransform() carries no crypto token; only a 2-pass link to the wrapper body (SymmetricAlgorithm.Create("RC2")) reveals RC2, and even there it is a string arg.

The type is referenced as SC.Aes via a namespace alias; AES isn't in the naive token list, and resolving the algorithm + 128-bit/CBC parameters needs structural binding of the SC alias and the KeySize/Mode assignments.

ECDSA reaches the source only through the ECDsa.Create(...) chain and the curve via ECCurve.CreateFromFriendlyName("nistP256"); no \bECDSA\b/\bRSA\b token, and the curve is a quoted friendly-name string.

RSA is created via AsymmetricAlgorithm.Create("RSA") string factory; the cast (RSA) is the only bare token and many token scanners skip cast expressions, while the 2048 key size is a runtime parameter not adjacent to any algorithm literal.

The compound transform string "DESede/CBC/PKCS7Padding" defeats a \bDES\b/DES(?![\w-]) guard (DESede is a different token), and the API CipherUtilities.GetCipher isn't in a System.Security.Cryptography-shaped token list.

The algorithm string is loaded from external config (config["Crypto:Hash"]) at runtime, so neither the algorithm token nor any literal appears in source; only dynamic analysis or config inspection resolves it.
The cipher lives only in the quoted compound string 'des-ede3-cbc'; a \bDES\b / DES(?![\w-]) guard never fires because the substring 'des' is glued to '-ede3-cbc'.
RC4 appears only as a lowercase quoted argument 'rc4'; a token scanner keyed on uppercase \bRC4\b or a struct keyword misses the lowercased cipher-spec string.
MD5 is a lowercased quoted literal first bound to $algo then passed to hash(); a \bMD5\b boundary scanner skips lowercase 'md5' and cannot follow the one-hop variable binding.
The hash context is bound to $h via hash_init('sha1') and consumed by hash_update/hash_final; the algorithm is a lowercase string and the digest call site never repeats a \bSHA-?1\b token.

No \bRSA\b token appears anywhere — the RSA operation is implied by openssl_public_encrypt + OPENSSL_PKCS1_PADDING and hidden behind the user function sealForRecipient(); only a 2-pass def-to-call link surfaces it.

snippet:
require 'openssl'
def legacy_cipher(key, iv)
  c = OpenSSL::Cipher.new('des-cbc')
  c.encrypt
  c.key = key
  c.iv = iv
  c
end
why_lexical_misses: The cipher name is the quoted argument 'des-cbc'; DES(?![\w-]) is defeated because '-cbc' follows 'des', and the Cipher object is then returned and used elsewhere with no algorithm token.

snippet:
require 'digest'
HASHER = Digest::MD5
def etag_for(body)
  HASHER.hexdigest(body)
end
why_lexical_misses: Digest::MD5 is aliased to the constant HASHER and the call site uses HASHER.hexdigest; a line-oriented \bMD5\b scan that flags only call sites (.hexdigest) never sees the algorithm on the same line.

snippet:
require 'openssl'
def issue_keypair
  bits = 2048
  key = OpenSSL::PKey::RSA.new(bits)
  [key, key.public_key]
end
why_lexical_misses: RSA appears as a class path segment (PKey::RSA) and the key size is the variable $bits=2048 not an inline literal; a scanner keyed on the bare \bRSA\b token may catch RSA but cannot resolve the 2048-bit strength through the local binding, mis-grading or under-reporting it.

snippet:
require 'digest'
ALGO = ENV.fetch('HASH_ALGO', 'SHA1')
def checksum(data)
  Digest.const_get(ALGO).hexdigest(data)
end
why_lexical_misses: The digest class is chosen at runtime via Digest.const_get(ENV['HASH_ALGO']); the only algorithm token is the default-fallback string in ENV.fetch, and the actual class is never named statically — a genuine reflection blind spot.

The active cipher is $ciphers[$mode] selected from a config value at runtime; neither 'Blowfish' nor a \bBF\b token exists ('bf-cbc' is an OpenSSL alias for Blowfish) and the chosen entry is undecidable statically.

snippet:
require 'openssl'
spec = %w[des ede3 cbc].join('-')
cipher = OpenSSL::Cipher.new(spec).tap(&:decrypt)
cipher.key = derive(pass)
why_lexical_misses: The cipher spec 'des-ede3-cbc' is assembled at runtime by joining a token array, so no contiguous cipher string or \bDES\b token exists in source; resolving it requires constant-folding the Array#join, beyond a lexical pass.
